results.possibleIdentity: Julius Kolin (1886–1976) was a distinguished aeronautical and mechanical engineer and consultant active in both Europe and the United States. Born in Brailov, Ukraine, he studied at the Technische Universität in Vienna. Around 1911, he tested innovative airplane propeller designs in Paris at the Laboratoire du Champ-de-Mars under the supervision of Gustave Eiffel. During World War I, he became a prominent figure in the Austro-Hungarian aviation industry, working as an engineer for Aviatik and the Flugzeug & Propellerfabrik (Fruba). He was a primary designer of the Aviatik D.I fighter and explored early concepts for vertical take-off aircraft. In December 1936, following the rise of the Nazi regime, he immigrated to the United States with his wife Malwina and their daughter, the artist Sacha Kolin, and settled in New York City. In his later career, Kolin worked as a consultant in aviation engineering; notably, he was credited with designing a tandem-bladed inducer for NASA under contract NAS 3-3728 in 1970. He was associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization in the late 1960s and early 1970s, where he was listed as a technical consultant for aviation engineering. His daughter Sacha, an early member of E.A.T., often cited her father's aeronautical legacy as a primary influence on her minimalist and geometric sculptures. Julius Kolin died in New York City in 1976.
